symptoms.symptom_name: Acute severe rest breathlessness on a background of decades of progressive exertional dyspnoea

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Progressive exertional dyspnoea culminating in acute rest dyspnoea is the core symptom of pulmonary venous congestion from mitral stenosis.
Haemoptysis in mitral stenosis reflects rupture of engorged bronchial/pulmonary venous anastomoses under raised pulmonary venous pressure.
Haemoptysis with acute dyspnoea also raises PE, which must be excluded on work-up.

symptoms.symptom_name: Orthopnoea - unable to lie flat, needs multiple pillows

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Orthopnoea is a hallmark of pulmonary venous congestion in left-sided/valvular heart failure.

symptoms.symptom_name: New fast, irregular heartbeat (symptomatic new atrial fibrillation)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: New rapid AF shortens diastolic filling across the stenotic valve, precipitating decompensation, and carries a high embolic risk.

symptoms.symptom_name: History of childhood rheumatic fever (predisposing valvular injury)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Childhood rheumatic fever is the principal cause of mitral stenosis and greatly raises pre-test probability.

symptoms.symptom_name: Profound fatigue and severe functional limitation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Low cardiac output and pulmonary congestion produce marked fatigue and exercise limitation.

symptoms.symptom_name: No fever and no purulent sputum

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of fever and purulent sputum argues against pneumonia and favours cardiogenic congestion.
Malar flush reflects low cardiac output with peripheral cyanosis and is a recognised sign of severe mitral stenosis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Loud S1, opening snap and low-pitched mid-diastolic apical rumbling murmur; bibasal crackles; irregularly irregular pulse; bilateral ankle oedema

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Decompensated rheumatic mitral stenosis with atrial fibrillation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The loud S1, opening snap and mid-diastolic apical rumble are the classic auscultatory triad of mitral stenosis; crackles, irregular pulse and oedema indicate decompensation with AF.

symptoms.symptom_name: ECG showing atrial fibrillation and echocardiography showing a stenosed, calcified/fused mitral valve

investigation_details.expected_result: ECG confirming atrial fibrillation; echocardiography showing a thickened, calcified, fused mitral valve with reduced valve area and a raised transvalvular gradient
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Severity of mitral stenosis and the presence/rate of atrial fibrillation cannot be quantified and definitive management cannot be planned.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Rheumatic mitral stenosis with atrial fibrillation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Echocardiographic mitral stenosis with AF on ECG confirms the diagnosis and quantifies severity.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Decompensated rheumatic mitral stenosis with new atrial fibrillation
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Acute rest dyspnoea and orthopnoea with haemoptysis, a new rapid irregular heartbeat and a childhood rheumatic fever history together give a high clinical suspicion of decompensated mitral stenosis precipitated by atrial fibrillation, mandating an emergency ambulance.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirms atrial fibrillation and ventricular rate, guiding urgent rate control.
Quantifies mitral valve area, gradient and calcification and assesses pulmonary pressures and left atrial size.
Assesses pulmonary venous congestion/oedema and left atrial enlargement.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Full blood count and inflammatory markers to pursue a chest infection over telehealth before escalation
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: This is a time-critical cardiopulmonary emergency; delaying an emergency ambulance to chase infection markers over telehealth is inappropriate.
